title: ‘Roma,’ ‘The Mule,’ and the Mailbag | The Oscars Show (Ep. 109)
format: Sean and Amanda open with Roma and The Mule, then work through a mailbag that swings from Bradley Cooper's Oscar hunger to the SAG ensemble nominations.

segments.heading: Roma and The Mule
segments.note: Two movies with nothing in common except they opened the same Friday

films.title: Roma
films.note: Alfonso Cuarón's Netflix release, in Spanish and black and white. Amanda calls it a phenomenal artistic achievement and thinks the theatrical-vs-Netflix conversation around it will run through the Oscars, at the movie's expense.

films.title: The Mule
films.note: Clint Eastwood's 40th film as director, playing an 88-year-old horticulturist turned drug runner for the Sinaloa cartel. Both hosts found it ridiculous and entertaining, cheerier than expected, and nowhere near the awards conversation.

films.title: A Star Is Born
films.note: The big-four question: Sean's pick for the category least likely to win is Best Director, since he thinks the Academy rewards Bradley Cooper in actor or picture instead. Also the jumping-off point for a question about whether being a fourth version of the story should count against it. Both hosts say no, nothing is original anyway.

films.title: The Wife
films.note: Glenn Close's Best Actress vehicle. Neither host has seen it, a running joke from the cold open, but they still rate her chances against Olivia Colman and Melissa McCarthy.

films.title: Fighting With My Family
films.note: Florence Pugh playing the wrestler Paige, written and directed by Stephen Merchant and produced by Dwayne Johnson. Amanda hasn't seen it yet but is curious given her interest in wrestling culture.

films.title: Little Women
films.note: Greta Gerwig's next film, with Florence Pugh as Amy March opposite Saoirse Ronan's Jo. Amanda is already counting down to the two of them sharing scenes.

films.title: Leave No Trace
films.note: Debra Granik's film with Thomasin McKenzie as the daughter of a man living off the grid, played by Ben Foster. Amanda calls McKenzie a real talent and wants to see her in something like a Star Wars movie next.

films.title: Ocean's Eight
films.note: Amanda's pick for most disappointing: a heist-at-the-Met-Gala cast that should have made it her favorite movie of all time, undercut by Steven Soderbergh not directing it. She calls it a good airplane movie and nothing more.

films.title: Assassination Nation
films.note: One of the loudest movies out of Sundance, bought big by Neon. Amanda found Sam Levinson's ideas about being a teenager under social-media surveillance interesting but the execution a mess, made by someone too far from that age to nail it.

films.title: The Predator
films.note: A Shane Black movie that Amanda, a fan of the whole series, found incoherent, and one that carried unfortunate behind-the-scenes news about a crew member's criminal record.

films.title: The Girl in the Spider's Web
films.note: Amanda had high hopes for Fede Alvarez taking the Stieg Larsson material somewhere grimier than David Fincher's version. It didn't happen.

films.title: Mary Queen of Scots
films.note: Amanda wanted a Saoirse Ronan and Margot Robbie head-to-head and instead got a lot of on-screen text explaining Scottish history. She admired it without thinking it fully worked, and blames some of that on herself for not knowing the history going in.

films.title: Vice
films.note: Christian Bale's Dick Cheney transformation, still under embargo at the time of the question. Both hosts think he's a lock for a nomination and neck and neck with Bradley Cooper, and both are surprised more by critics' lack of interest in the movie's untraditional structure than by its mixed reviews.

films.title: Eighth Grade
films.note: Bo Burnham's film comes up in the hypothetical Best Comedy category Amanda invents for the mailbag. She'd slot it in as a comedy on the strength of Burnham's identity and its funny moments, even though she calls it pure horror to her.

films.title: Game Night
films.note: Amanda's assumed lock for the made-up Best Comedy field: familiar enough to work as a studio comedy and a genuine word-of-mouth hit.

films.title: Crazy Rich Asians
films.note: Comes up as another contender for the hypothetical comedy category, and again later as a happy SAG Ensemble surprise.

segments.heading: The Big Race: Best Director
segments.note: Pivoting off the last mailbag question

films.title: Roma
films.note: Sean's example of a director doing everything himself: Cuarón wrote, directed, and shot the film, which Sean says almost nobody else at that level attempts.

films.title: BlacKkKlansman
films.note: Sean floats Spike Lee as a career-achievement winner here, not necessarily on this film's merits alone but on decades of never being nominated.

films.title: Can You Ever Forgive Me?
films.note: Marielle Heller is one of only two women Sean and Amanda expect on Gold Derby's director lists this year, alongside Debra Granik.

films.title: First Man
films.note: Damien Chazelle's film, cited as a master's work even though Amanda notes people aren't turning out to see it.

films.title: Green Book
films.note: Peter Farrelly's directing bid is, in Amanda's words, “a can of worms.”

films.title: If Beale Street Could Talk
films.note: Barry Jenkins's follow-up to Moonlight. Sean and Amanda talk through his close-up-heavy style, which their producer noticed right away watching it that weekend.

films.title: The Favourite
films.note: Yorgos Lanthimos gets a nod here for having a clear, recognizable point of view.

films.title: Black Panther
films.note: Sean hopes Ryan Coogler gets in for it.

films.title: Crazy Rich Asians
films.note: A stock-up pick for Amanda: a big successful Hollywood story that holds up on rewatch, with maybe an outside shot at Best Picture.

films.title: Bohemian Rhapsody
films.note: The nomination Amanda can't square: a movie she doesn't think has a great ensemble, riding pure Queen-song appeal to enormous box office despite being made partly by Bryan Singer.

films.title: Black Panther
films.note: One of the other Best Ensemble nominees, which Sean expects to also land a Best Picture nod.

films.title: BlacKkKlansman
films.note: Another expected Best Picture crossover nominee in the ensemble field.

films.title: A Star Is Born
films.note: The nominee Sean calls the closest thing to a lock for Best Picture among this group.

segments.heading: Next Week
segments.note: Two odd movies out this Friday

films.title: Bird Box
films.note: Susanne Bier's Netflix thriller starring Sandra Bullock, described by Sean as A Quiet Place but blind. He thinks people were holding a slot for it in the awards conversation and won't be anymore.

films.title: Welcome to Marwen
films.note: Robert Zemeckis's motion-capture film with Steve Carell, based on the documentary Marwencol. Sean can't explain why anyone turned that story into this movie, calls it one of the stranger misfires he's seen, and can't quite recommend it either.

referenced.group: Actors nominated twice in the same year, historically

films: To All the Boys I've Loved Before

The Girl in the Spider's Web.title: The Girl in the Spider's Web

To All the Boys I've Loved Before.title: To All the Boys I've Loved Before
